mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XE S engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned XE S (306PS AJ126) engine typically costs £5,000–£11,000 supply only. Fitting adds £900–£2,000 depending on workshop location. JLR have quoted £16,000 for parts only, while independent specialists quote around £10,500 for a second-hand engine fitted. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XE S engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ126 is a great engine, but its cooling system requires attention. Coolant warnings are extremely common and should never be ignored — they can lead to overheating, warped cylinder heads, or even full engine failure. The engine is also sensitive to oil sludge build-up, making frequent oil changes (every 8,000 miles) critical. Timing chain issues are less common than on the 5.0-litre V8 but can occur at higher mileage.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XE S?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system failures (water pump, plastic coolant pipes), supercharger coupler wear (rattling and 'clonk' on shutdown), and timing chain tensioner wear (rattle on cold start). The cooling system is the most common and urgent issue.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar XE S engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 60% Jaguar scrap threshold: if your XE S is worth £15,000–£20,000 and a recon engine is £7,000 (35–47%), it's worth it. If the vehicle is a tired £10,000 example, the same engine exceeds 50% — get a scrap valuation first. These are performance vehicles that often justify replacement spend.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar XE S engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The XE S uses the 306PS (also known as the AJ126) — a 3.0-litre supercharged V6 producing 340 bhp. It was fitted to the X760 XE from 2015 to 2019. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the cooling system, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system components can be repaired individually — water pump, coolant pipes, thermostat. The water pump costs under £200 from Jaguar. However, ignoring coolant warnings can lead to catastrophic engine failure, requiring a full replacement. Address any coolant leaks immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar XE S Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the only engine generation this badge ever used — the 306PS (AJ126). See the Era Map above for era-specific pricing. JLR have quoted £16,000 for parts only, while independent specialists quote around £10,500 for a second-hand engine fitted.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an XE S engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The tightly packed engine bay and complex supercharger system add significant labour time compared to simpler engines. The water pump replacement cost is around £300, with the pump itself under £200 from Jaguar.

cta.label: → Compare XE S engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Cooling System Failures — Water Pump & Coolant Pipes
problems.symptoms: Coolant level low warning, white smoke from engine bay, visible coolant leak, overheating, rattling noise from pump pulley.
problems.typicalMileage: 50,000–70,000 miles — failure reported at just 54,000 miles.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring coolant warnings can lead to catastrophic engine failure.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the coolant warning comes on, pull over immediately.

problems.recommendation: The cooling system is the Achilles' heel of the AJ126. The water pump is a weak point on the XE S, along with the plastic coolant pipes. Failure can be sudden and dramatic — one owner reported a huge cloud of white smoke from the engine bay when the water pump failed at 54,000 miles. The plastic cooling tee often bursts. If you see any coolant warnings, have the system inspected immediately. Many specialists recommend replacing the water pump and coolant pipes as a precautionary measure.

problems.title: Supercharger Coupler Wear
problems.symptoms: Diesel-like ticking and rattling from the engine, loud "clonk" from the top of the engine when turning off, rattling that sounds like "marbles in a tin can" — loudest on cold start, decreasing as the engine warms up.
problems.repairCost: £400–£800 (supercharger coupler replacement). Not expensive if you change it yourself.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if left for many thousands of miles, supercharger performance will degrade.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if you hear rattling or the "clonk".

problems.recommendation: The supercharger coupler/torsion isolator is a known wear item on the AJ126. The good news is that a worn coupler is normally nothing to worry about and is just annoying. However, if it is left for many thousands of miles, it will eventually wear so much that supercharger performance will degrade. If you hear the characteristic "clonk" when turning off the engine or a "marbles in a tin can" rattle on cold start, have the coupler inspected.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling timing chains — much more regular and higher pitched than a coupler rattle, worse with a cold engine. Can eventually lead to a stretched or skipped chain, causing pistons to hit valves and destroying the engine.
problems.typicalMileage: 100,000+ miles — normally they go near 100k miles, but early cars can suffer from 57,000 miles.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if the chain snaps, it's an interference engine — catastrophic failure requiring full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: at first sign of metallic rattle on cold start, book inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The AJ126 is nowhere near as bad for timing chain failure as the early 5.0-litre AJ133 V8, but timing chains can still be a problem. The tensioners wear a hole in the guides. The number one enemy of these engines is oil sludge build-up. To prevent timing chain issues, change the engine oil far more often than the scheduled 16,000-mile interval — roughly every 8,000 miles. If you hear a regular, high-pitched rattle on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: XE S — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Simple fix — do it immediately to avoid overheating
Well-known weak point — repair when symptoms appear
Preventative — catches fault before catastrophic failure
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This XE S? Check These First
buyIf: * Water pump and coolant pipes recently replaced — the most critical maintenance item
* No coolant warnings or evidence of coolant leaks
* Full service history with oil changes every 8,000 miles or less (not the 16,000-mile interval)
* Supercharger coupler inspected and confirmed healthy
avoidIf: * Coolant warning lights or evidence of leaks — cooling system issues are common
* Rattling from the engine — could be timing chain or supercharger coupler wear
* Poor service history — these engines are sensitive to oil sludge build-up
* Overheating or coolant loss — can cause warped cylinder heads or full engine failure

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The AJ126 is a great engine when properly maintained, but its cooling system requires attention and it is sensitive to oil sludge build-up. The water pump and plastic coolant pipes are known weak points. Coolant warnings are extremely common and should never be ignored. A £7,000 reconditioned engine on an XE S valued at £15,000–£20,000 is 35–47% of the car's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. On a sub-£10,000 example, think carefully. The 60% Jaguar scrap threshold applies.

cta.label: → Compare XE S engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar XE S (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Coolant Level — Check for low coolant or visible leaks — the most common issue
* Coolant Warning — Any intermittent "Coolant Level Low" warnings?
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling on startup (timing chain or supercharger coupler wear)
* Supercharger Noise — Any "clonk" when turning off? "Marbles in a tin can" rattle?
* Service History — Oil changes every 8,000 miles (not the 16,000-mile interval)
* Engine Bay — Check for coolant residue or crusted coolant on hoses
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
